purpose: Explains why G3 central bank balance sheet normalization paths are qualitatively different exercises, not different speeds of the same process
components: Fed: sovereign currency issuer with deep, liquid capital markets — constraints are distributional and political, not structural
ECB: manages a currency union where member states are effectively currency users — QT is a political act as much as monetary, TPI conditionality creates path dependency
BoJ: holds >50% of JGBs and effectively IS the JGB market — normalization means creating a market where none exists, a categorically different challenge
Classification: these represent different categories of central bank, not different positions on a single spectrum
Implication: cross-G3 averaging of balance sheet metrics is analytically meaningless
current_assessment: Unchallenged in any debate. Implies duration absorption stress concentrates disproportionately in USD-denominated markets given ECB political constraints and BoJ market dominance. Most analytically productive when used to reject naive cross-G3 comparisons. Does not address non-G3 central banks (PBoC, RBI) which may be equally consequential for certain markets.
limitations: Framework is descriptive — does not predict specific normalization endpoints for any G3 central bank
ECB TPI conditionality is untested in practice under actual QT stress
BoJ market creation challenge may be theoretically impossible, not merely difficult
Does not address non-G3 central banks (PBoC, RBI) whose balance sheets may be more consequential for commodity and EM asset markets
Political constraints are inherently unpredictable and may shift rapidly
